You're signed in. Your data is loaded fr: You're signed in. Your data is loaded from the database.
Set Duration and Speed, then use Step to: Set Duration and Speed, then use Step to learn slowly.
Use Run to watch the full path and Save: Use Run to watch the full path and Save to keep results.
Market Regime changes the “behavior patt: Market Regime changes the “behavior pattern” of returns: drift (average direction), volatility (swing size), and rare shock events. Your inputs stay the same; only the market behavior changes.
Balanced: typical long-run growth with m: Balanced: typical long-run growth with moderate swings.
Defensive/Bearish: lower drift with deep: Defensive/Bearish: lower drift with deeper drawdowns.
Choppy & volatile: bigger swings even if: Choppy & volatile: bigger swings even if averages look similar.
Stress test: rare crash + recovery behav: Stress test: rare crash + recovery behavior.
Compare “Total you put in” vs portfolio: Compare “Total you put in” vs portfolio value to see break-even. Real value shows purchasing power after inflation.
Arrow tip: under the big numbers, <span: Arrow tip: under the big numbers, green ↑ means that number went up since the previous step, and red ↓ means it went down. The chart legend lines are separate.
This page creates a saved scenario. You: This page creates a saved scenario. You can always tweak behavior later on the run page.
Market Regime is the market “mood”: it c: Market Regime is the market “mood”: it changes drift, volatility, and shock behavior. Try the same inputs under different regimes to see drawdowns and recovery differences.
Small changes in risk or market influenc: Small changes in risk or market influence can drastically change drawdowns. Save and compare multiple scenarios instead of trying to “perfect” one.
Market Regime is chosen on the run page. It changes market behavior (drift/volatility/shocks) while keeping your scenario inputs the same.
Investment simulations are for education: Investment simulations are for educational purposes only. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
Get market insights and platform updates: Get market insights and platform updates delivered to your inbox
mail.2fa_disabled_subject: Your two-factor authentication (2FA) has been disabled
mail.2fa_disabled_body: Your account recovery request has been reviewed. Two-factor authentication (2FA) has been disabled for your account.
mail.2fa_disabled_footer: You can sign in with your password. If anything looks wrong, use the reset link below or contact support.
mail.2fa_disabled_links_plain: If the buttons do not work, copy these links into your browser:
mail.2fa_disabled_auto: This message was sent automatically by :app.
mail.account_blocked_subject: Your email address has been blocked on this platform
mail.account_blocked_body: The email address :email can no longer be used to register, sign in, or request password resets on this site. This action was taken because the account activity violated the platform rules.
mail.account_blocked_appeal: If you believe this is a mistake, you may submit a support ticket to appeal the decision. Include any details that help us verify your account.
mail.account_blocked_auto: This message was sent automatically by :app.
You cannot remove your own administrator: You cannot remove your own administrator privileges.
mail.test_body:
This is a test message from :app.

Mail driver: :mailer
From address: :from

If you received this in your inbox, SMTP (or your configured driver) is working.
If MAIL_MAILER=log, look in storage/logs instead of your inbox.
mail.test_warn_log: MAIL_MAILER is "log" — the message was written to the application log, not sent to an inbox. Set MAIL_MAILER=smtp and fill MAIL_* for real delivery.
mail.password_reset_initiated_subject: Password reset requested for your account
mail.password_reset_initiated_body: Someone just requested a password reset for this email address on our site (for example from the Support page or the “Forgot password” form). A separate email contains the link to choose a new password.
mail.password_reset_initiated_your_messa: What you wrote when you requested the reset:
mail.password_reset_initiated_if_you: If this was you, you can ignore this message and use the reset link in the other email when you are ready.
mail.password_reset_initiated_if_not: If you did not request this, use the button below. We will cancel pending reset links for your account so no one can change your password with an old email. You can still sign in with your current password.
mail.password_reset_initiated_btn_not_me: I did not request this — cancel reset links
mail.password_reset_initiated_footer: If the button does not work, copy the long link under it into your browser. For more help, visit the Support page.
mail.password_reset_initiated_auto: This message was sent automatically by :app.

Account recovery help text: If you lost access to your authenticator (2FA), you can request help here. Add a description so the admin can verify your request, and include the email you used for your account.
2FA recovery description placeholder: Explain that you lost 2FA access and what happened. Include any helpful details.
Support hub intro: Submit bug reports or account issues while signed in, or request help if you cannot access your account (including lost 2FA).

Account access & recovery: Can't sign in or lost two-factor authentication?
Password reset mail log notice: Email is configured to log only (local dev). Open storage/logs/laravel.log and search for your address: you should see two messages — a short security notice (with a “this was not me” link) and the email that contains the actual reset link. For a real inbox, set MAIL_MAILER=smtp in .env and add your SMTP settings.
Password reset support explainer: You will get two emails: first a short security notice (with a button if this was not you), then the message with the link to set a new password. If you came from Support, your optional note below is included in the security email so you remember what you asked for.
Password reset optional message label: Optional note (shown in the security email)
Password reset optional message placehol: Example: “Locked out after travel; requesting reset from Support.”
Password reset optional message hint: Only used when you start reset from Support. Max 2000 characters.
Password reset from support intro: We can email you a link to set a new password. You will also receive a short security email: if you did not ask for a reset, you can cancel pending reset links in one click.
Password reset unauthorized acknowledged: Pending password reset links for your account have been cancelled. If you still need access, you can request a new reset from Support or the login page.

tutorial.title: Simulation tutorial
tutorial.dashboard.0: You’ll build intuition by running a scenario, stepping month-by-month, and comparing nominal value (account balance) vs real value (purchasing power after inflation).
tutorial.dashboard.1: Welcome! This tutorial will teach you how the investment simulator works and how to use it for learning. You will create a scenario, run it month-by-month, and compare nominal value (money amount) vs real value (purchasing power after inflation).
tutorial.dashboard.2: This is your simulations list. Each row is one saved scenario. Use it to open a simulation and continue experimenting, or edit/delete a scenario you no longer need.
tutorial.dashboard.3: Create a new simulation here. You’ll set a starting amount, monthly contributions, and assumptions (expected growth, inflation, risk and market influence). Then you’ll run it and learn how outcomes change.
tutorial.dashboard.4: Open a simulation to run it month-by-month. If you don’t have one yet, create your first simulation first.
tutorial.create.0: Tip: use realistic ranges. Many long-term equity assumptions are 5–9% growth and 1–3% inflation. Higher risk or market influence will increase volatility and drawdowns.
tutorial.create.1: Give your simulation a clear name (example: “ETF plan 10 years”, “High risk test”). A good name helps you remember what you were testing and compare scenarios later.
tutorial.create.2: Initial Investment is your starting balance at month 0. Think of it as the money you already have invested today.
tutorial.create.3: Monthly Contribution is how much you invest every month. In real life, consistent contributions often matter more than trying to time the market.
tutorial.create.4: Annual Growth Rate is your long‑term expected return (percent per year). This is an average assumption; real markets move up and down around that average.
tutorial.create.5: Inflation Rate is the yearly increase in prices. The simulator uses it to show “real value”, so you learn the difference between account balance and purchasing power.
tutorial.create.6: Risk Appetite (0–100%) controls how strong the month‑to‑month swings can be. Higher risk means bigger ups and downs and deeper drawdowns.
tutorial.create.7: Market Influence (0–100%) controls how much the simulation reacts to external market shocks. Higher influence makes outcomes more chaotic (more realistic), especially in volatile regimes.
tutorial.create.8: Click Create to save the scenario. After creating, open it and use Step mode first—watch how each month affects contributions, growth, drawdown, and inflation-adjusted value.
tutorial.show.0: Goal: keep the chart and the key numbers visible at the same time. Use the right panel to control the simulation, and watch how the chart responds.
tutorial.show.1: This is your simulation run page. You can run the scenario quickly or step month‑by‑month. Use it to understand compounding, volatility, drawdowns, and inflation.
tutorial.show.1b: Run, Pause, Step, Reset, and Save stay on a top bar so they are always visible next to the status—no overlapping menus.
tutorial.show.2: These controls change how the simulator runs. For learning, start slow, use Step for a few months, then Run for a longer horizon (10–30 years).
tutorial.show.3: Duration is how many months to simulate. 120 months = 10 years. 360 months = 30 years. Longer horizons usually reduce the impact of short-term volatility.
tutorial.show.4: Speed controls how fast the simulation advances when running. It does not change the math—only how quickly you see results.
tutorial.show.5: Market Regime changes the type of market behavior (steady, bullish, defensive, volatile, crash+recovery). Try the same settings under different regimes to see risk and drawdowns.
tutorial.show.5b: Second scenario overlays another path on the same chart: for example the same monthly returns but in reverse order (sequence-of-returns risk), or the same path with extra monthly contributions. Use it to compare decisions, not to predict markets.
tutorial.show.6: Run Simulation advances month-by-month automatically. You can pause anytime. Use this to see the full path, not only the final number.
tutorial.show.7: Step advances by one month. This is the best learning tool: observe how contributions are added, how returns fluctuate, and when drawdowns happen.
tutorial.show.8: Save sends the latest snapshot and a monthly history to the server only when you press it—use it after runs you care about so you can reconstruct the chart later.
tutorial.show.9: The chart shows portfolio value (nominal), total contributed (dashed—break-even vs profit), and real (inflation-adjusted) value with a shaded band between nominal and real. Optional lines show a comparison or reversed-return path. Pausing may show a vertical marker on the current month.
tutorial.show.10: Current Value is the latest portfolio value in the simulation. It changes from contributions plus market returns (including good and bad days).
tutorial.show.11: Total Contributed is how much money you put in (initial + monthly contributions). Comparing Current Value vs Contributed helps you understand true gains.
tutorial.show.12: Real Value adjusts the portfolio for inflation. A portfolio can grow in nominal terms but still lose purchasing power if inflation is high.
tutorial.show.13: Max Drawdown is the worst peak-to-trough drop. It’s a key risk concept: big drawdowns can take years to recover from, even if the long-term average return is good.
tutorial.show.13b: CAGR (compound annual growth rate) summarizes how fast the portfolio grew on average over the simulated period. It complements drawdowns: you can have a decent CAGR and still have lived through painful dips along the way.
tutorial.show.14: Market Events & Teaching Moments explains notable events (shocks, new highs, drawdowns). Use it like a coach: it helps connect the numbers to behavior and risk management.
tutorial.show.15: Exercise: set Duration to 360 months (30 years), choose “Stress test”, then Step 12 months. Notice how contributions keep working even when returns are negative. Then Run the rest and compare nominal vs real value.
Save the latest simulation results to yo: Save the latest simulation results to your dashboard
Tip: keep the chart visible while you ad: Tip: keep the chart visible while you adjust controls. Use Step for learning; Run for long horizons.
This app is designed to help you build i: This app is designed to help you build intuition. Run scenarios, step month-by-month, and compare nominal growth vs inflation-adjusted purchasing power.
Use Step mode to see exactly what happen: Use Step mode to see exactly what happens each month. Then Run to understand longer horizons (10–30 years).
Volatility matters. The simulator highli: Volatility matters. The simulator highlights drawdowns so you learn why staying consistent is hard — and why it matters.
A bigger number is not always a better o: A bigger number is not always a better outcome. Compare nominal value to real value after inflation.
Create a scenario, run it, and save snap: Create a scenario, run it, and save snapshots so you can compare outcomes.
Set an initial investment, monthly contr: Set an initial investment, monthly contributions, and assumptions like growth and inflation.
Use market regimes (balanced, growth, de: Use market regimes (balanced, growth, defensive, volatile, stress test) to explore different paths.
Save progress, review events, and compar: Save progress, review events, and compare multiple scenarios to understand trade-offs.
If you’re new, start with the Quick Tour: If you’re new, start with the Quick Tour and then create your own simulation.
No notable events yet. Run or step the s: No notable events yet. Run or step the simulation.
New portfolio high reached in month :mon: New portfolio high reached on day :month.
Preset: :label: Preset: :label
Balanced portfolios rely on regular cont: Balanced portfolios rely on regular contributions and modest volatility. Focus on time in the market.
Growth tilt: higher expected return but: Growth tilt: higher expected return but bigger swings. Stick to a plan when volatility hits.
Defensive stance tempers losses but can: Defensive stance tempers losses but can lag in bull markets. Contributions matter more.
Choppy markets teach discipline: expect: Choppy markets teach discipline: expect whiplash and focus on long-term averages.
Stress test simulates a crash and recove: Stress test simulates a crash and recovery. Diversification and time horizon matter most.
Stay invested and watch how contribution: Stay invested and watch how contributions and volatility interact.
High risk appetite means larger swings. Keep an emergency fund outside this simulation.
Strong market influence toggled: externa: Strong market influence toggled: external shocks will matter more. Rebalance if needed.
Inflation is elevated; compare nominal v: Inflation is elevated; compare nominal vs real value to see purchasing power.
Inflation is moderate; compounding still: Inflation is moderate; compounding still beats it over time.
Use Step mode to see how each month cont: Use Step mode to see how each month contributes to long-term results.
Two-factor authentication secret is miss: Two-factor authentication secret is missing. Please reload the setup page and try again.
Two-factor authentication secret is inva: Two-factor authentication secret is invalid. Please reload the setup page and try again.

Requirements: Minimum 12 characters, at: Requirements: Minimum 12 characters, at least one uppercase letter, one lowercase letter, and one number or symbol (!@#$%^&*)
No problem. Enter your email and we will: No problem. Enter your email and we will send a reset link.
Buy adds money into the investment. Sell: Buy adds money into the investment. Sell moves part of the investment back to your cash wallet. The orange line is profit/loss compared to how much you put in (it can go below 0 = loss).
Close this panel to make the chart wider: Close this panel to make the chart wider. Open it to buy/sell.
The starting amount you invest in euros. This is your initial capital before any growth or contributions.
The amount you add to your investment ea: The amount you add to your investment each month. Regular contributions help your portfolio grow through compound interest.
Expected annual return in percent (0-100: Expected annual return in percent (0-100). Example: 7% annual growth. Higher rates mean more potential gains but also more risk.
How much volatility you are comfortable: How much volatility you are comfortable with (0-100%). Higher values mean your investment will have bigger swings up and down, simulating a more aggressive strategy.
How much external market factors affect: How much external market factors affect your simulation (0-100%). Higher values add more realistic market fluctuations, making the simulation more dynamic and unpredictable.
The annual inflation percentage (0-100). Example: 2% inflation. This helps you see the real purchasing power of your investment over time, accounting for price increases.
Number of investors in this scenario. Mo: Number of investors in this scenario. More investors can amplify crowd behavior (profit-taking and panic waves), especially with high Market Influence.
Use Step or Run in the bar above: each m: Use Step or Run in the bar above: each month reprices your holdings and redraws the chart.
This page is being built step-by-step. N: This page is being built step-by-step. Next we’ll add the shared market, weekly coins, and a leaderboard.
Hands-on desk hint: Opens the trading desk beside the chart. You can also select “Hands-on portfolio lab” under Controls.
Sell part of holdings (priced at current: Sell part of holdings (priced at current day)
Added :amount — more shares at current p: Added :amount — more shares at current price.
Sold :pct% of position. This trade P/L:: Sold :pct% of position. This trade P/L: :gain.
Try selling after a peak versus after a: Try selling after a peak versus after a dip to see how timing changes locked-in gains. This is a teaching model, not live trading.
Heavy selling from other investors weigh: Heavy selling from other investors weighed on prices (~:pct% crowd drag).
Broad buying from other investors helped: Broad buying from other investors helped prices (~:pct% crowd lift).
When closed, the chart is wider. Tap to: When closed, the chart is wider. Tap to open buy/sell.
When closed, the chart is wider. Hover o: When closed, the chart is wider. Hover or focus here to expand buy/sell.
Optional detail — open for numbers and m: Optional — open for numbers and month-over-month hints
